**Hoodoo Gurus** are an Australian [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") band formed in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney") in 1981[[4]](#cite_note-McF-4) by [Dave Faulkner](/wiki/Dave_Faulkner_(musician) "Dave Faulkner (musician)") (songwriter, lead singer and guitarist) and later joined by [Richard Grossman](/wiki/Richard_Grossman_(bassist) "Richard Grossman (bassist)") (bass), Mark Kingsmill (drums), and [Brad Shepherd](/wiki/Brad_Shepherd "Brad Shepherd") (guitar, vocals, harmonica).[[5]](#cite_note-OzRockDb-5) Their popularity peaked in the mid- to late 1980s with albums *[Mars Needs Guitars!](/wiki/Mars_Needs_Guitars! "Mars Needs Guitars!")*, *[Blow Your Cool!](/wiki/Blow_Your_Cool! "Blow Your Cool!")* and *[Magnum Cum Louder](/wiki/Magnum_Cum_Louder "Magnum Cum Louder")*.

Hoodoo Gurus had a string of pop-rock singles including "[Leilani](/wiki/Leilani_(song) "Leilani (song)")" (1982), "[Tojo](/wiki/Tojo_(song) "Tojo (song)")" (1983), "[My Girl](/wiki/My_Girl_(Hoodoo_Gurus_song) "My Girl (Hoodoo Gurus song)")" (1983), "[I Want You Back](/wiki/I_Want_You_Back_(Hoodoo_Gurus_song) "I Want You Back (Hoodoo Gurus song)")" (1984), "[Bittersweet](/wiki/Bittersweet_(Hoodoo_Gurus_song) "Bittersweet (Hoodoo Gurus song)")", "[Like Wow – Wipeout](/wiki/Like_Wow_%E2%80%93_Wipeout "Like Wow – Wipeout")" (1985), and "[What's My Scene?](/wiki/What%27s_My_Scene%3F "What's My Scene?")" (1987).[[4]](#cite_note-McF-4) After touring the United States from 1984 onward they gained popularity on the U.S. [college rock](/wiki/College_rock "College rock") circuit with the singles "[Come Anytime](/wiki/Come_Anytime "Come Anytime")" (1989)[[6]](#cite_note-6) reaching no. 1 and "[Miss Freelove '69](/wiki/Miss_Freelove_%2769 "Miss Freelove '69")" (1991)[[7]](#cite_note-Freelove-7) reaching No. 3 on the [Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)") [Modern Rock Tracks](/wiki/Hot_Modern_Rock_Tracks "Hot Modern Rock Tracks") chart.[[8]](#cite_note-Billboard-8) The Hoodoo Gurus' biggest Australian single was their 1987 top-3 song "What's My Scene?".[[9]](#cite_note-Best87-9) The song was parodied for the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") 2000s theme "That's My Team".[[10]](#cite_note-Kent-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Melb-11)

The Hoodoo Gurus were inducted into the Australia's 2007 [ARIA Hall of Fame](/wiki/ARIA_Hall_of_Fame "ARIA Hall of Fame").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-HoF-13) The band's induction announcement stated that the Gurus were one of the most "inventive, lyrically smart and exciting" bands from Australia.[[14]](#cite_note-ARIA2007-14) From 1960s power pop and garage punk to hard rock and funky psychedelic kitsch, the group's music stood out from Sydney's [Detroit](/wiki/Music_of_Detroit "Music of Detroit")-inspired bands.[[4]](#cite_note-McF-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hoodoo_Gurus&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1981–1983: Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hoodoo_Gurus&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1981–1983: Formation")]

Three Hoodoo Gurus founders were from Perth [protopunk](/wiki/Protopunk "Protopunk") and [punk](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") bands: Dave Faulkner (guitar) of The Legendary Manikins, who left after a fallout with frontman Robbie Porritt, [James Baker](/wiki/James_Baker_(musician) "James Baker (musician)") (drums) of [The Victims](/wiki/The_Victims_(Perth_band) "The Victims (Perth band)"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) who had also been with [Roddy Radalj](/wiki/Roddy_Radalj "Roddy Radalj") (aka Roddy Ray'Da) (guitars)[[16]](#cite_note-RR-16) in [The Scientists](/wiki/The_Scientists "The Scientists"),[[4]](#cite_note-McF-4)[[17]](#cite_note-17) and the third founding member, [Kimble Rendall](/wiki/Kimble_Rendall "Kimble Rendall") (guitars) was formerly in Sydney punk rockers [XL Capris](/wiki/XL_Capris "XL Capris").[[4]](#cite_note-McF-4)[[18]](#cite_note-18) The three guitarists Faulkner, Rendall and Radalj met at an end-of-1980 New Year's Eve party and were joined by Baker to form **Le Hoodoo Gurus**.[[19]](#cite_note-Harp-19)

Le Hoodoo Gurus unorthodox line-up of three guitars and no [bass](/wiki/Bass_guitar "Bass guitar") player created a unique sound that fused pop melodies, punk guitars and an American trash culture ethic.[[4]](#cite_note-McF-4) This was captured on their first single, "[Leilani](/wiki/Leilani_(song) "Leilani (song)")", released in October 1982, on [Phantom Records](/wiki/Phantom_Records "Phantom Records").[[20]](#cite_note-20) This told the story of a maiden sacrificed to the gods and an erupting volcano while her true love looked on helplessly.[[4]](#cite_note-McF-4) They dropped the 'Le', to become Hoodoo Gurus, not long after the single's release.[[4]](#cite_note-McF-4) As the Hoodoo Gurus, they gave their first ever performance on a segment of the kids' TV program *[Simon Townsend's Wonder World](/wiki/Simon_Townsend%27s_Wonder_World "Simon Townsend's Wonder World")*; this was followed by a performance alongside a man and his singing dog, on *[The Don Lane Show](/wiki/The_Don_Lane_Show "The Don Lane Show")*.[[21]](#cite_note-Howlspace-21)

Rendall left in 1982 before the release of "Leilani", and went on to become a [music video](/wiki/Music_video "Music video") and film director.[[21]](#cite_note-Howlspace-21)[[22]](#cite_note-IMDbKR-22) Rendall was second unit director on the latter two [Matrix](/wiki/The_Matrix_(series) "The Matrix (series)") films, *[The Matrix Reloaded](/wiki/The_Matrix_Reloaded "The Matrix Reloaded")* and *[The Matrix Revolutions](/wiki/The_Matrix_Revolutions "The Matrix Revolutions")*, as well as *[I, Robot](/wiki/I,_Robot_(film) "I, Robot (film)")*. Rendall also directed the [slasher film](/wiki/Slasher_film "Slasher film"), *[Cut](/wiki/Cut_(2000_film) "Cut (2000 film)")* in 2000.[[22]](#cite_note-IMDbKR-22) Rendall was effectively replaced by Clyde Bramley (bass guitar, backing vocals) from Sydney bands [The Hitmen](/wiki/The_Hitmen "The Hitmen") and Super K.[[4]](#cite_note-McF-4)[[5]](#cite_note-OzRockDb-5)

Radalj was not happy with Rendall's leaving or Faulkner's greater influence and left the Gurus.[[19]](#cite_note-Harp-19) He was replaced by ex-Fun Things guitarist [Brad Shepherd](/wiki/Brad_Shepherd "Brad Shepherd"), who had been Bramley's flatmate.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Bramley and Shepherd had both been in The Hitmen and Super K.[[4]](#cite_note-McF-4)[[24]](#cite_note-OzRockDbBradShep-24) Faulkner wrote "[I Want You Back](/wiki/I_Want_You_Back_(Hoodoo_Gurus_song) "I Want You Back (Hoodoo Gurus song)")" in response to Radalj's public dissatisfaction with the Gurus.[[25]](#cite_note-Pop-25) Radalj went on to perform with a number of other acts, including [The Johnnys](/wiki/The_Johnnys "The Johnnys"), Love Rodeo, [The Dubrovniks](/wiki/Dubrovniks "Dubrovniks") and Roddy Ray'Da & the Surfin' Caesars.[[16]](#cite_note-RR-16) As Roddy Ray'Da, he released *Lost, Lonely and Vicious* in 1990, *Orgazmatazz* a year later and *Mouthful of Chicken* in 1993.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### 1983–1984: *Stoneage Romeos*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hoodoo_Gurus&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1983–1984: Stoneage Romeos")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Drummer_Mark_Kingsmill_with_Hoodoo_Gurus.jpg/250px-Drummer_Mark_Kingsmill_with_Hoodoo_Gurus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Drummer_Mark_Kingsmill_with_Hoodoo_Gurus.jpg)

Drummer Mark Kingsmill with Hoodoo Gurus

Gurus new line-up (Baker, Bramley, Faulkner and Shepherd) recorded the band's first album in 1984, *[Stoneage Romeos](/wiki/Stoneage_Romeos "Stoneage Romeos")*.[[5]](#cite_note-OzRockDb-5) The title came from a 1955 [Three Stooges](/wiki/Three_Stooges "Three Stooges") [short](/wiki/List_of_Three_Stooges_shorts "List of Three Stooges shorts") *[Stone Age Romeos](/wiki/Stone_Age_Romeos "Stone Age Romeos")*. The album was dedicated to characters from *[Get Smart](/wiki/Get_Smart "Get Smart")*, *[F-Troop](/wiki/F-Troop "F-Troop")* and *[Petticoat Junction](/wiki/Petticoat_Junction "Petticoat Junction")*.[[4]](#cite_note-McF-4) They were awarded 'Best Debut Album' of 1984 at the July 1985 *[Countdown](/wiki/Countdown_(Australian_TV_series) "Countdown (Australian TV series)")* Music Awards.[[27]](#cite_note-Count85-27) When *Stoneage Romeos* was released in America it stayed at number 1 in the Alternative/College charts for seven weeks, becoming one of the most played albums for the year on the college network.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Initially a cult inner-city act, their popularity expanded due to regular airplay on radio station [Triple J](/wiki/Triple_J "Triple J") and nationwide pop TV show *Countdown* from mid-1983. Their breakthrough single "[My Girl](/wiki/My_Girl_(Hoodoo_Gurus_song) "My Girl (Hoodoo Gurus song)")" was accompanied by a video clip featuring a dog trainer with his once champion [greyhound](/wiki/Greyhound "Greyhound"). Members of [Spiderbait](/wiki/Spiderbait "Spiderbait") described seeing the video for the first time as "a beautiful, classic pop song".[[29]](#cite_note-29) Some viewers insisted the song was "written about a dog".[[25]](#cite_note-Pop-25) This was closely followed by "[I Want You Back](/wiki/I_Want_You_Back_(Hoodoo_Gurus_song) "I Want You Back (Hoodoo Gurus song)")", which featured [animated](/wiki/Stop_motion "Stop motion") plastic model [dinosaurs](/wiki/Dinosaur "Dinosaur"). Both videos were aired frequently raising the group's profile around the country.[[21]](#cite_note-Howlspace-21)

Original drummer James Baker was sacked from the band in August 1984[[4]](#cite_note-McF-4)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Hollow-31) and was replaced by Mark Kingsmill ([The Hitmen](/wiki/The_Hitmen "The Hitmen"), [New Christs](/wiki/New_Christs "New Christs"), [Screaming Tribesmen](/wiki/Screaming_Tribesmen "Screaming Tribesmen"), Hellcats and Super K).[[5]](#cite_note-OzRockDb-5) Based on the success of *[Stoneage Romeos](/wiki/Stoneage_Romeos "Stoneage Romeos")* the Hoodoo Gurus, with their new drummer, then embarked on their first tour of the United States in late 1984.[[19]](#cite_note-Harp-19)

### 1985–1993: *From Mars* to *Kinky*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hoodoo_Gurus&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1985–1993: From Mars to Kinky")]

Following the US tour the Hoodoo Gurus went into the studio and recorded their second album, *[Mars Needs Guitars!](/wiki/Mars_Needs_Guitars! "Mars Needs Guitars!")* (1985). The title was a twist on a [Sci Fi](/wiki/Sci_Fi "Sci Fi") [B movie](/wiki/B_movie "B movie") titled *[Mars Needs Women](/wiki/Mars_Needs_Women "Mars Needs Women")*. The album's first single, "[Bittersweet](/wiki/Bittersweet_(Hoodoo_Gurus_song) "Bittersweet (Hoodoo Gurus song)")", had a more subdued regular-looking video. The Hoodoo Gurus played Europe and the United States including sell-out shows at [London](/wiki/London "London")'s [Hammersmith Palais](/wiki/Hammersmith_Palais "Hammersmith Palais") and a two-month tour as special guests with [The Bangles](/wiki/The_Bangles "The Bangles").[[25]](#cite_note-Pop-25)

Baker's firing upset some fans and critics. Despite subsequent fan speculation about the single "[Poison Pen](/wiki/Poison_Pen_(song) "Poison Pen (song)")”, songwriter Faulkner said it was not about band issues but rather about a relationship that had turned bitter.[[25]](#cite_note-Pop-25) Baker had been involved with [The Beasts of Bourbon](/wiki/The_Beasts_of_Bourbon "The Beasts of Bourbon") as a side project from 1983[[31]](#cite_note-Hollow-31) and now concentrated on their 1984 album *The Axeman's Jazz*, subsequently he founded James Baker Experience and [The Dubrovniks](/wiki/Dubrovniks "Dubrovniks") (latter two included Radalj), Novakill and eventually The Painkillers.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Gurus' peak of popularity was in the mid-to-late 1980s with the albums *[Mars Needs Guitars!](/wiki/Mars_Needs_Guitars! "Mars Needs Guitars!")*, *[Blow Your Cool!](/wiki/Blow_Your_Cool! "Blow Your Cool!")* and *[Magnum Cum Louder](/wiki/Magnum_Cum_Louder "Magnum Cum Louder")*. The albums reached no. 140 (1986), no. 120 (1987) and no. 101 (1989) on the American [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200") album charts respectively.[[33]](#cite_note-Billboard_Albums-33) The band's 1987 'Blow Your Cool' concert tour took them to 19 countries playing 204 venues. A concert from the Ritz in New York City was broadcast by [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV") America. The 'Magnum Cum Louder' tour in 1990, with dates in Europe, America and Australasia; included Japan for the first time. Based on their extensive touring of America, the band gained a solid following in the U.S. with regular exposure on US college radio (and later [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV")'s [120 Minutes](/wiki/120_Minutes "120 Minutes")) and even developed a big fan base in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil").[[13]](#cite_note-HoF-13)[[25]](#cite_note-Pop-25) The third album *Blow Your Cool!* let go of some of the peripheral quirkiness and just concentrated on the band's pop power. Members of their international peer group [The Bangles](/wiki/The_Bangles "The Bangles") and [Dream Syndicate](/wiki/Dream_Syndicate "Dream Syndicate") contributed backing vocals to some songs. Then, Hoodoo Gurus decided to opt out of its record contract, tying up the group in legal wranglings for more than a year. In 1988 [Richard Grossman](/wiki/Richard_Grossman_(bassist) "Richard Grossman (bassist)") (ex [Matt Finish](/wiki/Matt_Finish "Matt Finish"), [Divinyls](/wiki/Divinyls "Divinyls"))[[34]](#cite_note-OzRockDbGross-34) replaced Clyde Bramley on bass.[[4]](#cite_note-McF-4) The most stable line-up of Faulkner, Grossman, Kingsmill, and Shepherd saw Hoodoo Gurus from 1988 to the break-up in January 1998.[[35]](#cite_note-GClarke-35)

After two albums under their new contract, *Magnum Cum Louder* and 1991's *[Kinky](/wiki/Kinky_(Hoodoo_Gurus_album) "Kinky (Hoodoo Gurus album)")*, which reached no. 101 and 172 on the American [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200") album charts respectively[[33]](#cite_note-Billboard_Albums-33) the band then released in 1992 two compilations at once, *[Electric Soup](/wiki/Electric_Soup_(album) "Electric Soup (album)")*, which contained the band's hits and *[Gorilla Biscuit](/wiki/Gorilla_Biscuit "Gorilla Biscuit")*, which was made up of B-sides and rarities.

*Kinky* provided the single "[Miss Freelove '69](/wiki/Miss_Freelove_%2769 "Miss Freelove '69")" which reached no. 19 on the [ARIA](/wiki/Australian_Recording_Industry_Association "Australian Recording Industry Association") Singles Chart,[[7]](#cite_note-Freelove-7) and no. 3 on the [Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_charts "Billboard charts") [Modern Rock Tracks](/wiki/Modern_Rock_Tracks "Modern Rock Tracks") chart in 1991. Another single of the same year "[1000 Miles Away](/wiki/1000_Miles_Away "1000 Miles Away")" reached no. 37 in Australia.[[36]](#cite_note-36) It was adopted by the crew of [Royal Australian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Navy "Royal Australian Navy") frigate [HMAS *Canberra*](/wiki/HMAS_Canberra_(FFG_02) "HMAS Canberra (FFG 02)") as its unofficial anthem in 1993, playing it whenever its ships left port.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Hoodoo Gurus played a concert, including "1000 Miles Away", on-board HMAS *Canberra* during its last voyage prior to decommissioning in 2005.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Crank through to the Bullet (1994–1998)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hoodoo_Gurus&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Crank through to the Bullet (1994–1998)")]

They followed *Kinky* with *[Crank](/wiki/Crank_(Hoodoo_Gurus_album) "Crank (Hoodoo Gurus album)")* in 1994. The 'Crank' world tour took the Gurus through USA, Canada, Europe and included a one-month tour of [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil").[[4]](#cite_note-McF-4) Faulkner also composed the soundtrack to Laurie McInnes' 1994 feature film *Broken Highway*.[[39]](#cite_note-39) In 1995, the Gurus collaborated with [The Masters Apprentices](/wiki/The_Masters_Apprentices "The Masters Apprentices") frontman [Jim Keays](/wiki/Jim_Keays "Jim Keays") on an updated rendition of "Turn Up Your Radio". The song was included on a new Masters' Best of album, as well as being issued as a single.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

In 1996 they moved to [Mushroom Records](/wiki/Festival_Mushroom_Records "Festival Mushroom Records") for the release of their next album, *[Blue Cave](/wiki/Blue_Cave_(Hoodoo_Gurus_album) "Blue Cave (Hoodoo Gurus album)")*. The band then undertook a national tour of Australian tour with US outfit [The Posies](/wiki/The_Posies "The Posies") and local band Drop City. Touring commitments were curtailed when Kingsmill severed tendons and nerves in his arm as a result of an onstage accident.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Once Kingsmill recovered, the Gurus completed their 'Circus Maximus' Australian tour by end of 1996. In January 1997, Faulkner announced that the band would be breaking up in December. March saw the Gurus undertake another tour of [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), where they played a concert in front of 40,000 people. The Gurus commenced their final Australian tour, the three-month 'Spit the Dummy' tour, in October 1997. The tour coincided with the release of the double compilation album *[Armchair Gurus](/wiki/Armchair_Gurus "Armchair Gurus")*/*[Electric Chair](/wiki/Electric_Chair_(album) "Electric Chair (album)")*, which made no. 29 on the national chart. The band played its final show in Melbourne on 11 January 1998.[[35]](#cite_note-GClarke-35)[[42]](#cite_note-42) The show made up for dates missed during December 1997, when Faulkner was ill.[[35]](#cite_note-GClarke-35)

In November 1998 [Mushroom](/wiki/Mushroom_Records "Mushroom Records") issued the Hoodoo Gurus' live album, *[Bite the Bullet](/wiki/Bite_the_Bullet_(Hoodoo_Gurus_album) "Bite the Bullet (Hoodoo Gurus album)")*. The songs had been recorded during the band's 'Spit the Dummy' farewell tour. The standard, single album version was joined by a [limited edition](/wiki/Limited_edition "Limited edition"), triple CD version subtitled *Director’s Cut*. It included the additional CDs, *Doppelgänger* (a collection of live-to-air Broadcasts from between 1983 and 1996) and *Bubble and Squeak* (a collection of outtakes and oddities).[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Although Hoodoo Gurus officially split in 1998 they signed with Acadia Records, who released the band's career-spanning compilation *[Ampology](/wiki/Ampology "Ampology")* in October 2000.

### 1998–2003: Hoodoo hiatus – side projects and solo work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hoodoo_Gurus&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1998–2003: Hoodoo hiatus – side projects and solo work")]

#### Grossman

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hoodoo_Gurus&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Grossman")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Richard_Grossman%2CApril_2012.jpg/250px-Richard_Grossman%2CApril_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Richard_Grossman,April_2012.jpg)

Grossman, April 2012

Main articles: [Ghostwriters (band)](/wiki/Ghostwriters_(band) "Ghostwriters (band)") and [Richard Grossman (bassist)](/wiki/Richard_Grossman_(bassist) "Richard Grossman (bassist)")

From 1990 onwards, Grossman has been involved in a side project, [Ghostwriters](/wiki/Ghostwriters_(band) "Ghostwriters (band)"), with [Rob Hirst](/wiki/Rob_Hirst "Rob Hirst") from [Midnight Oil](/wiki/Midnight_Oil "Midnight Oil").[[34]](#cite_note-OzRockDbGross-34) [Ghostwriters](/wiki/Ghostwriters_(band) "Ghostwriters (band)") have released four albums with 2007's *Political Animal* on [Sony BMG](/wiki/Sony_BMG "Sony BMG"). Shepherd had played with Ghostwriters on their albums *Ghostwriters* in 1991 and *Fibromoon* in 1999.[[24]](#cite_note-OzRockDbBradShep-24)[[34]](#cite_note-OzRockDbGross-34) During 2001, Grossman performed 20 concerts with long-time friend Matt Moffitt (1956–2003) singer-songwriter and founder of [Matt Finish](/wiki/Matt_Finish "Matt Finish"). Grossman had been an early member of [Matt Finish](/wiki/Matt_Finish "Matt Finish"),[[34]](#cite_note-OzRockDbGross-34) he returned to perform and record *Just a Short Note (Best of)* released in December 2001.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) The Kelly Gang was a 2004 project Grossman formed with Jack Nolan, they brought in Hirst and Scott Aplin (Waikiki) to record *Looking for the Sun*; Shepherd performed as a session musician.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

#### Shepherd

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hoodoo_Gurus&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Shepherd")]

Main article: [Brad Shepherd](/wiki/Brad_Shepherd "Brad Shepherd")

Shepherd was briefly involved with [Beasts of Bourbon](/wiki/Beasts_of_Bourbon "Beasts of Bourbon") alongside fellow Guru, James Baker.[[24]](#cite_note-OzRockDbBradShep-24) He recorded their four-track CD, *From the Belly of the Beasts*, a live 1984 performance at the Trade Union Club, Sydney.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Shepherd's tenure with Beasts ended after Baker was sacked from the Gurus.[[48]](#cite_note-48) He joined another ex-Guru, Roddy Radalj, in Roddy Ray'Da and the Surfin' Caesars during 1989.[[24]](#cite_note-OzRockDbBradShep-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Guitarist_and_singer-songwriter_Brad_Shepherd_backstage_at_Hotel_Rottnest%2C_April_2012.jpg/250px-Guitarist_and_singer-songwriter_Brad_Shepherd_backstage_at_Hotel_Rottnest%2C_April_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guitarist_and_singer-songwriter_Brad_Shepherd_backstage_at_Hotel_Rottnest,_April_2012.jpg)

Guitarist and singer-songwriter Shepherd backstage at Hotel Rottnest, April 2012

In 1999, following Gurus break-up, Shepherd recorded "[Alex Chilton](/wiki/Alex_Chilton_(song) "Alex Chilton (song)")" for [The Replacements](/wiki/The_Replacements_(band) "The Replacements (band)") tribute album, *I'm in Love... With That Song* (Antfarm Records).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] He also produced [Shutterspeed](/wiki/Shutterspeed "Shutterspeed")'s debut 1999 EP, *Up Go The Shutters* and co-produced their second EP, *Well Ain't That Something A Good Thing's Comin'*  on Laughing Outlaw Records. Shepherd went on to form a new band, The Monarchs (initially known as The Honkies), together with Andy Kelly on bass (Glide), Greg Hitchcock on guitar (The Neptunes, [The Kryptonics](/wiki/The_Kryptonics "The Kryptonics"), New Christs, The Verys, Challenger 7, [You Am I](/wiki/You_Am_I "You Am I"), [The Dearhunters](/wiki/The_Dearhunters "The Dearhunters"))[[49]](#cite_note-49) and his brother Murray Shepherd (Harpoon, The Fun Things, [Screaming Tribesmen](/wiki/Screaming_Tribesmen "Screaming Tribesmen")) on drums. The Monarchs played with [The Meanies](/wiki/The_Meanies "The Meanies"), [Even](/wiki/Even_(band) "Even (band)"), [You Am I](/wiki/You_Am_I "You Am I"), [Joe Strummer](/wiki/Joe_Strummer "Joe Strummer") and [The Hellacopters](/wiki/The_Hellacopters "The Hellacopters").[[50]](#cite_note-50) In August 1999 The Monarchs recorded a self-financed album with [Wayne Connolly](/wiki/Wayne_Connolly "Wayne Connolly") and in 2000 they released their first single, "2001" b/w "This Is All I Can Do" ([Ivy League Records](/wiki/Ivy_League_Records "Ivy League Records")); this was followed by their debut album, *Make Yer Own Fun*, in October 2001, on [Shock Records](/wiki/Shock_Records "Shock Records").[[24]](#cite_note-OzRockDbBradShep-24)

#### Faulkner

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hoodoo_Gurus&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Faulkner")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Dave_Faulkner_performing_at_Hotel_Rottnest_on_April_2012.jpg/250px-Dave_Faulkner_performing_at_Hotel_Rottnest_on_April_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dave_Faulkner_performing_at_Hotel_Rottnest_on_April_2012.jpg)

Faulkner at Hotel Rottnest, April 2012

Main article: [Dave Faulkner (musician)](/wiki/Dave_Faulkner_(musician) "Dave Faulkner (musician)")

Antenna was Faulkner's side project (1998–1999) collaborating with long-time friend [Kim Salmon](/wiki/Kim_Salmon "Kim Salmon") (The Cheap Nasties, [The Manikins](/wiki/The_Manikins "The Manikins"), [The Scientists](/wiki/The_Scientists "The Scientists"), [Beasts of Bourbon](/wiki/Beasts_of_Bourbon "Beasts of Bourbon")), also in Antenna were Justin Frew and Stuart McCarthy (both of [Southend](/wiki/Southend_(band) "Southend (band)")). Antenna recorded an album mid-1998, and then made its live debut at the Telstra Concert of the Century/[Mushroom](/wiki/Mushroom_Records "Mushroom Records") 25th anniversary in November 1998. The concert coincided with the release of the band's debut single, "Come on Spring", and album, *Installation*.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Guest performers on the album included Matt Thomas (The Mavis's) on "All Rise", and [Christina Amphlett](/wiki/Christina_Amphlett "Christina Amphlett") ([Divinyls](/wiki/Divinyls "Divinyls")) on "Divine". "Come on Spring" reaching no. 64 on the 1998 [Triple J Hottest 100](/wiki/Triple_J_Hottest_100 "Triple J Hottest 100") list. Antenna appeared on the 1999 [Big Day Out](/wiki/Big_Day_Out "Big Day Out") tour but disbanded soon after.

Faulkner also wrote original music for the films *Broken Highway* (1993) and *[The Sum of Us](/wiki/The_Sum_of_Us_(film) "The Sum of Us (film)")* (1994) starring [Russell Crowe](/wiki/Russell_Crowe "Russell Crowe") and [Jack Thompson](/wiki/Jack_Thompson_(actor) "Jack Thompson (actor)").[[52]](#cite_note-52) Faulkner was one of the new entries in the 2007 edition of *[Who's Who in Australia](/wiki/Who%27s_Who_in_Australia "Who's Who in Australia")*.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

#### Persian Rugs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hoodoo_Gurus&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Persian Rugs")]

In September 2001, the four ex-Gurus (Faulkner, Grossman, Kingsmill & Shepherd) performed as The Moops and were later called Persian Rugs.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-Homebake-55) At [Homebake](/wiki/Homebake "Homebake") 2001, (8 December) both Hoodoo Gurus and Persian Rugs performed separate sets.[[55]](#cite_note-Homebake-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) Persian Rugs recorded a five-track EP *Mr. Tripper* in June 2002, Grossman then left, and Shepherd recommended bassist Kendall James as his replacement[[57]](#cite_note-57) (ex Thurston Howlers, Crusaders).[[58]](#cite_note-58) With James, the Persian Rugs recorded their debut album *Turkish Delight*, released in August 2003.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

Persian Rugs provided the track "Be My Guru" for the Hoodoo Gurus' tribute album *Stoneage Cameos* (2005)[[60]](#cite_note-60) (see *[Stoneage Romeos](/wiki/Stoneage_Romeos "Stoneage Romeos")*) and by that time Hoodoo Gurus, with Grossman on bass, had already reformed.

> The Persian Rugs was me doing a complete '60s revival sort of thing. Also, in a funny way, it was my own sort of version of retaliating at all the critics who always harped on the Gurus' '60s influences: "You want to hear what I do when I do '60s? Here it is!" But I also had a whole lot of songs after the Gurus broke up, and though I rehearsed them with different musicians, they just couldn't seem to get the flavor of what I was trying to do. It became obvious to me that there was only one band that could play these songs the way I wanted to hear them. So *Mach Schau*, far from us coming back and being "middle aged" and writing for an older fan base, we just wanted to make a hard rocking record that out-did anything we'd ever done. We really blew a gasket on that one—it's our *Presence*, like Led Zeppelin.

— Dave Faulkner, [[19]](#cite_note-Harp-19)

### 2003–2012: Regroup and ARIA Hall of Fame

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hoodoo_Gurus&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2003–2012: Regroup and ARIA Hall of Fame")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Richard_Grossman_and_Brad_Shepherd_from_Hoodoo_Gurus.jpg/250px-Richard_Grossman_and_Brad_Shepherd_from_Hoodoo_Gurus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Richard_Grossman_and_Brad_Shepherd_from_Hoodoo_Gurus.jpg)

Grossman and Shepherd

By early 2003 Hoodoo Gurus had re-formed to record "That's My Team" as the promotional theme for the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") between 2003 and 2007 (a CD was released September 2003 – see "[What's My Scene?](/wiki/What%27s_My_Scene%3F "What's My Scene?")") with all profits donated to [breast cancer](/wiki/Breast_cancer "Breast cancer") charities.[[61]](#cite_note-61) The original film clip of "What's My Scene?" included shots of band members in [Wests](/wiki/Western_Suburbs_Magpies "Western Suburbs Magpies") and [Cronulla](/wiki/Cronulla_Sharks "Cronulla Sharks") jumpers.

On 17 November 2003 [EMI](/wiki/EMI "EMI") Records announced Hoodoo Gurus' reformation to record a new album, *[Mach Schau](/wiki/Mach_Schau_(Hoodoo_Gurus_album) "Mach Schau (Hoodoo Gurus album)")*; they also released the track "White Night" as a radio-only single.[[62]](#cite_note-62) In January 2004 the band co-headlined the annual [Big Day Out](/wiki/Big_Day_Out "Big Day Out") festival with [Metallica](/wiki/Metallica "Metallica") and [The Strokes](/wiki/The_Strokes "The Strokes").[[63]](#cite_note-63) *Proudly Australian – celebrate Australia Day 2004* which included "Nothing's Changing My Life" by Hoodoo Gurus was a four-track give-away CD.[[64]](#cite_note-64) "Nothing's Changing My Life" (February 2004) was released as a single with *Mach Schau* following in March.[[65]](#cite_note-65) The 'Mach Schau' tour started in Perth late in March and was joined by [Spiderbait](/wiki/Spiderbait "Spiderbait") from April to June.[[66]](#cite_note-66) EMI reissued expanded/remastered editions of all Hoodoo Gurus albums (from February 2005); also released was *Tunnel Vision* (2005), a two-DVD set compiling every Gurus' video, never-before-seen live material and a retrospective documentary, "Be My Guru".

The band performed at the [2006 NRL Grand Final](/wiki/2006_NRL_Grand_Final "2006 NRL Grand Final").

In 2007, Hoodoo Gurus was inducted into the [ARIA Hall of Fame](/wiki/ARIA_Hall_of_Fame "ARIA Hall of Fame").[[13]](#cite_note-HoF-13) The band were inducted by [HG Nelson](/wiki/Greig_Pickhaver "Greig Pickhaver") of [Triple J](/wiki/Triple_J "Triple J")'s *[This Sporting Life](/wiki/This_Sporting_Life_(radio_program) "This Sporting Life (radio program)")*.[[67]](#cite_note-67) They performed "[The Right Time](/wiki/The_Right_Time_(Hoodoo_Gurus_song) "The Right Time (Hoodoo Gurus song)")" and "[I Want You Back](/wiki/I_Want_You_Back_(Hoodoo_Gurus_song) "I Want You Back (Hoodoo Gurus song)")" at the ceremony and subsequently toured with fellow inductees [Radio Birdman](/wiki/Radio_Birdman "Radio Birdman").[[68]](#cite_note-68)

The citation for the band's nomination includes:

> For two and a half decades the Hoodoo Gurus have consistently been one of the most inventive, lyrically smart and exciting rock'n'roll bands Australia has ever produced... along the way they've influenced an entire generation of bands which explains why the likes of [You Am I](/wiki/You_Am_I "You Am I"), [The Living End](/wiki/The_Living_End "The Living End"), [Dallas Crane](/wiki/Dallas_Crane "Dallas Crane"), [Grinspoon](/wiki/Grinspoon "Grinspoon") and many others queued up a few years back to pay tribute to the band's 1984 debut album, *[Stoneage Romeos](/wiki/Stoneage_Romeos "Stoneage Romeos")*

— ARIA.[[13]](#cite_note-HoF-13)

Hoodoo Gurus toured during 2007 including several US dates,[[69]](#cite_note-69) kicking off at the [South By Southwest](/wiki/South_By_Southwest "South By Southwest") (SXSW) Music Festival in [Austin, Texas](/wiki/Austin,_TX "Austin, TX")[[70]](#cite_note-70) (see SXSW photo above), performances in Europe, including the [Azkena](/wiki/Vitoria-Gasteiz "Vitoria-Gasteiz") Festival (Spain).[[71]](#cite_note-71) This was followed by a national tour of Australia called 'Clash of the Titans' with [The Stems](/wiki/The_Stems "The Stems") and [Radio Birdman](/wiki/Radio_Birdman "Radio Birdman").[[72]](#cite_note-72) Their tour poster depicts the three bands as 'Heavyweights' of the Australian music scene. Another solo tour started late in 2007 (including performances at the [Apollo Bay](/wiki/Apollo_Bay "Apollo Bay") Music Festival,[[73]](#cite_note-73) [Splendour in the Grass](/wiki/Splendour_in_the_Grass "Splendour in the Grass"),[[74]](#cite_note-74) [Meredith Music Festival](/wiki/Meredith_Music_Festival "Meredith Music Festival"),[[75]](#cite_note-75) and the [St Kilda Festival](/wiki/St_Kilda_Festival "St Kilda Festival")[[76]](#cite_note-76)) and continued into 2008.[[77]](#cite_note-77) There are tentative plans to release a new album in 2009, although little material has been written so far.[[78]](#cite_note-78) In June 2008 [The Age](/wiki/The_Age "The Age") newspaper commemorated 50 years of Australian rock 'n' roll (the anniversary of the release of [Johnny O'Keefe](/wiki/Johnny_O%27Keefe "Johnny O'Keefe")'s "[Wild One](/wiki/Wild_One_(Johnny_O%27Keefe_song) "Wild One (Johnny O'Keefe song)")") by selecting the Top 50 Australian Albums, with *Stoneage Romeo* coming in at no. 5 on the list.[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80) The Hoodoo Gurus performed at the [Glastonbury Festival](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival_2008 "Glastonbury Festival 2008") in the United Kingdom on 29 June 2008.[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82)

Hoodoo Gurus performed on 14 March 2009 for [Sound Relief](/wiki/Sound_Relief "Sound Relief") at the [Sydney Cricket Ground](/wiki/Sydney_Cricket_Ground "Sydney Cricket Ground").[[83]](#cite_note-Mitchell-83) Sound Relief was a benefit concert for victims of the [Victorian Bushfire Crisis](/wiki/February_2009_Victorian_bushfires "February 2009 Victorian bushfires") and the [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") [Floods](/wiki/Flood "Flood").[[84]](#cite_note-Brumby-84) Appearing with Hoodoo Gurus at the Sydney concert were, [Coldplay](/wiki/Coldplay "Coldplay"), [Eskimo Joe](/wiki/Eskimo_Joe "Eskimo Joe"), [Icehouse](/wiki/Icehouse_(band) "Icehouse (band)"), [Jet](/wiki/Jet_(band) "Jet (band)"), [Josh Pyke](/wiki/Josh_Pyke "Josh Pyke"), [Little Birdy](/wiki/Little_Birdy "Little Birdy"), [The Presets](/wiki/The_Presets "The Presets"), [Wolfmother](/wiki/Wolfmother "Wolfmother"), [You Am I](/wiki/You_Am_I "You Am I") and additional artists.[[85]](#cite_note-Official-85)

In March 2009 the Hoodoo Gurus also signed a new recording contract with [Sony Music Australia](/wiki/Sony_Music_Australia "Sony Music Australia"), the deal includes the band's back catalogue as well as a new album, the band's ninth and the first since the release of *Mach Schau* in 2004.[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87) Brad Shepherd said it had the potential to be "a career defining record".[[88]](#cite_note-hdg-88) During the process of writing the album Dave Faulkner said in reference to the eclectic mix of songs ready for the album that "rather than stopping myself writing something, I just didn't artificially force myself to write in any direction".[[88]](#cite_note-hdg-88) The album was originally scheduled for release in September 2009 but they were not happy with the final mix.[[89]](#cite_note-WA-89)

> We weren't really satisfied with the studio we were in and a whole lot of things, so we had to pull the plug on that and then rethink. We just had to eat the expense, you know, we had to give them a week's cancellation (fee).

— Dave Faulkner[[89]](#cite_note-WA-89)

Hoodoo Gurus then sent their songs off to [Durango, Colorado](/wiki/Durango,_Colorado "Durango, Colorado"), where [Ed Stasium](/wiki/Ed_Stasium "Ed Stasium"), who worked on the band's previous albums *Kinky* and *Crank* worked with Faulkner. Faulkner flew to the US after Hoodoo Gurus performed in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"),[[90]](#cite_note-90) their first performances there in over 20 years.[[89]](#cite_note-WA-89)

In August 2009 it was revealed that Shepherd had been diagnosed with [cancer](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer") and was recovering from recent surgery.[[91]](#cite_note-DT-91) It was his second cancer diagnosis, having had a [melanoma](/wiki/Melanoma "Melanoma") removed five years earlier.[[91]](#cite_note-DT-91)

The first single from their new album, "Crackin' Up", was released in December 2009[[92]](#cite_note-92) and received some airplay on Australian radio stations.[[93]](#cite_note-93) The album, *[Purity of Essence](/wiki/Purity_of_Essence_(Hoodoo_Gurus_album) "Purity of Essence (Hoodoo Gurus album)")*, was released on 16 March 2010 in Australia and internationally on 11 May 2010.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

On 3 October 2010, Hoodoo Gurus toured to London, UK, performing at O2 Shepherd's Bush Empire.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

In 2011, they were inducted into the [Music Victoria Hall of Fame](/wiki/Music_Victoria_Awards "Music Victoria Awards").[[96]](#cite_note-96)

In April 2012 the band played a series of concerts titled Dig it Up! in Australian capital cities, to celebrate 30 years since the release of their debut single "[Leilani](/wiki/Leilani_(song) "Leilani (song)")". At the concerts, the band performed the whole of their debut album *[Stoneage Romeos](/wiki/Stoneage_Romeos "Stoneage Romeos")* and other hits. Also playing as part of the Dig It Up! tour were Australian bands [Died Pretty](/wiki/Died_Pretty "Died Pretty") and [The Hard Ons](/wiki/The_Hard_Ons "The Hard Ons"), as well as international bands such as [Redd Kross](/wiki/Redd_Kross "Redd Kross"), [The Sonics](/wiki/The_Sonics "The Sonics") and [The Fleshtones](/wiki/The_Fleshtones "The Fleshtones").

### 2013–present: Continued performances and *Chariot of the Gods*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hoodoo_Gurus&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2013–present: Continued performances and Chariot of the Gods")]

In April 2013, the band hosted invitational concerts across the Australian capital cities, performing Mars Needs Guitars, and will be joined by [Blue Öyster Cult](/wiki/Blue_%C3%96yster_Cult "Blue Öyster Cult"), [Flamin' Groovies](/wiki/Flamin%27_Groovies "Flamin' Groovies"), [Buzzcocks](/wiki/Buzzcocks "Buzzcocks"), and [Peter Case](/wiki/Peter_Case "Peter Case").

2014 saw the release of *[Gravy Train](/wiki/Gravy_Train_(EP) "Gravy Train (EP)")*, an EP featuring three new songs and a remake of their first single "Leilani".

In January 2015, it was announced that Kingsmill would be retiring from the band. His last performances were on 22 and 23 May 2015, at the "Be My Guru – Evolution Revolution" concerts at Sydney's [Powerhouse Museum](/wiki/Powerhouse_Museum "Powerhouse Museum"), performed as the opening to the city's annual [Vivid Festival](/wiki/Vivid_Sydney "Vivid Sydney"). The concerts reunited all eight past and present band members for a two-hour set covering their beginnings as Le Hoodoo Gurus in 1982 all the way through to 2010's *Purity of Essence*.

The band headlined the 'A Day on the Green' concert series in March 2016, with [Sunnyboys](/wiki/Sunnyboys "Sunnyboys"), [Violent Femmes](/wiki/Violent_Femmes "Violent Femmes"), [Died Pretty](/wiki/Died_Pretty "Died Pretty"), and [Ratcat](/wiki/Ratcat "Ratcat").[[97]](#cite_note-97)

In early 2018, the group announced a vinyl reissue campaign called the Hoodoo Gurus Record Club, entailing the reissue on coloured vinyl of their back catalogue up to *Purity of Essence*, which also included the album the group recorded as the Persian Rugs in 2000. The first release through the campaign was *Stoneage Romeos* on 1 June 2018, with a general public release on 7 September.

On 26 May 2018, the group held a benefit concert for the [Tathra bushfire](/wiki/2018_Tathra_bushfire "2018 Tathra bushfire") with artists [The Badloves](/wiki/The_Badloves "The Badloves"), [1927](/wiki/1927_(band) "1927 (band)") and local talents Daniel Champagne, Corey Legge, The Figmentz, Erin McMahon, and [Melanie Horsnell](/wiki/Melanie_Horsnell "Melanie Horsnell"). The 'Band Together' concert sold out and ticket sales helped raise over half of the $250,000 target[[98]](#cite_note-98) towards helping residents affected by the bushfire in March 2018.

> We all saw the terrible fires and what they did to people's lives. We are people with empathy and wanted to do what we could.

— Dave Faulkner

[[99]](#cite_note-99) On 13 January 2019, Hoodoo Gurus headlined the 'Under the Southern Stars' concert tour of five states starting off at Hastings, Victoria, with performances in Tasmania, Queensland, New South Wales and South Australia scheduled throughout January. Appearing with Hoodoo Gurus at the Hastings concert were [Eskimo Joe](/wiki/Eskimo_Joe "Eskimo Joe"), [You Am I](/wiki/You_Am_I "You Am I"), [The Superjesus](/wiki/The_Superjesus "The Superjesus"), British India, The Getaway Plan and Scott Darlow.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

In July 2020, the band released the single "Hung Out to Dry". The song features on the vinyl version of the Gurus' tenth studio album, *[Chariot of the Gods](/wiki/Chariot_of_the_Gods_(album) "Chariot of the Gods (album)")*,[[101]](#cite_note-101) released in March 2022.
